speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: My life in the Werkdorp Nieuwesluis. I have chosen to become a night watchman there, and my duties were to go around to be sure there were no fire-- no fires. Because there were many little wooden barracks, where the young boys and young girls slept, and there was a big house where general things took place. I had to be sure that the fires in the-- the-- the stoves in the barracks were out. And was a night watchman. Didn't do anything. Studying, learning, playing chess, listen to good music, and studying, studying. And at 5:00 in the morning, I had to wake up the boys and the girls who had service milking the cows. It was a terrible thing to go in those barracks where there were seven or eight girls sleeping, and wake two or three of them-- wake up two or three of them who had early kitchen service or-- or milking service. It was very bad. And I met many guys there who were a little but older than I am, who had partially college education. And I would say, I have always referred to-- and I have said it many times in Holland after the war-- that Werkdorp Nieuwesluis for me was a mini university, a mini college. That is where I did 80% to 90% of my self-educating. I fell in love with a wonderful Austrian girl by the name of Sonja Reich. She was born 1919, so she was three years younger, so I was-- no, six years younger. So I was 22 and she was about 16. I was 23, and she was 17. And she fell very much in love with her-- with me, I mean. And I fell very much in love with her. No point in going into details. Now, a terrible thing occurred, and it was the first time in my life that I had an opportunity to do something wonderful. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In January, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1940, Sonja got a use certificate for Aliyah to Israel. The war was five months old-- started in August; we are now in January-- and she did not want to go to Israel in January, '40, because she wanted to be with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I somehow sensed or believed that eventually, the Nazis will Germany. Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean Holland, which actually happened on May 10, 1940. So I tried every type of persuasion on Sonja to accept her possibility to go to Israel and she didn't. I called her very bad names, which I cannot repeat here. I did everything possible to push her away from me. Didn't. So I beat her up. She left. And that was the first time in my life that I did something great and put her interest over-- for me, to l-- lose her was terrible, by the way. But I felt that I-- she had to go because she could save her life. And probably, she couldn't do it in Holland. Until today, she is very much in my heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Sonja the young lady you became engaged to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That was afterwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you were still in this camp. Who-- who was running-- who was running the camp that-- that you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The camp was, at that time, run by an Israeli gentlemen by the name of Moise Katznelson, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with whom I had a tremendous fight and won. We had a soccer team. I was-- I was only interested before I came to the camp, where-- which was my college, with gambling, girls, and soccer. Nothing else really interested me. And we had a soccer team there, and we were playing a little bit around. And I said, I'd like our soccer team to become part of the Dutch Soccer Association. I translated. It's the Nederland [SPEAKING DUTCH], the Dutch Soccer [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Organization. And Moise Katznelson, who had a very Eastern European-- Eastern Jewish European mentality, said, no way. It will create problems. And if our soccer team goes into the little villages around-- by they way, Nieuwesluis-- Werkdorp Nieuwesluis-- was all the way in the north of Holland, next to a small city by the name of Schagen, about two hours by train away from Amsterdam. And Katznelson, no way. It will create problems. It will create problems with the population of the area. I say, you don't know what you're talking about because nothing unites people more than sport, and art, and science. It will make us popular. People will like us. It will be wonderful. He didn't do it. But I did a few-- did a little bit politicking and the soccer team of the Jewish Werkdorp Nieuwesluis became part of the Dutch Soccer Association. That took place in middle '39. Let's say July, August, September, that area. And we-- every Sunday, you know, we went to various communities in the area for the game. It was C class, not A or B class. And that was my victory, by the way. It's nothing to do with what we are talking about, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but it was an organization that you were involved in, in a very trying time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was right, by the way. It endeared-- no-- no, not endeared. It brought us close to many of the rural population in that area, and that helped a great deal the years after in the underground situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on a day-to-day basis while you were in this camp, what were you hearing about Hitler and what was going on in Europe? What news, wh-- what information were you getting?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister and my mother-in-law, who were living in Lisbon, they gave me-- what you call it-- a subscription to a German-- not a German-- to a Swiss newspaper by the name of Weltwoche, which is a weekly periodically from Zurich, Switzerland. I got it every week. And I learned an awful lot, because they were telling in many, many details of the things which were going on in Poland, during the short war and after. Don't forget, we are talking now-- we are now in early '40. And there were the-- the-- [? destruction ?] camps did not exist. But nevertheless, ten thousands of Jews were executed and murdered by machine gun fire and put into certain synagogues, which they put on fire. I mean, it was all-- I knew what was going on through that particulars newspaper. And that newspaper, I got for a long time because, after all, Switzerland was a neutral country. And at that time, Holland was not occupied yet. Occupation only started in May, 1940. That is when my subscription was stopped, of course. And I was in contact with the world through BBC-- da da da dum-- beautiful theme of Beethoven's symphony. And that's where my knowledge came from.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on a day-to-day basis, were you-- were you working outside-- out-- outside of your-- of your camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Working? No. But we did this. We went swimming at the-- the North Sea, in-- well, I don't think makes any sense. The name of that little place was Callantsoog. They were two hours by bicycle. We had close contact with the population. We went on trips and we worked in the camp and once in a while, we got furloughed to Amsterdam, some people. Well, not Amsterdam. Everyone would go. I would say, during the time when I was in Werkdorp, for two years and two months, I probably was in Amsterdam maybe five, six times, seven times, the two or three day furlough.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you feel antisemitism while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after? Why did you leave the camp? What were the circumstances that you left the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't leave the camp. Now, let's talk about May 10, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is when, early in the morning, the bombing started. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Werkdorp Nieuwesluis was very close to Den Helder, which was the harbor of the Dutch Navy. And it was a tremendous bombardment and almost everybody woke up. And my friend Gunther [? Schoneberg, ?] he woke me up. I was told that later on by him. He said, Eric, get up. It is war. And I said, let me sleep. There will we war tomorrow yet. Let me sleep. Everything changed, naturally, after May 14th. I mean, Holland surrendered on May 14th. And the situation became very, very difficult. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we still stayed in the camp till March, 1941. In other words, we stayed in Nieuwesluis-- in Werkdorp Nieuwesluis-- till March, '41. And on March '41, all of a sudden, about 12 buses came-- big buses. And there were stormtroopers in Gestapo [? interpasses ?] and deciding [INAUDIBLE]. And all the boys and girls of the Werkdorp, they had to come to whatever you call it. And were were about-- we were about 400, about 250 boys and 150 girls, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Gestapo told us we all have to go in-- into the buses, and we are taken to Amsterdam. Then the technical leaders of the camp-- they were not Jewish-- they said, well, you cannot take the-- all those people away because the cattle-- someone has to take care of the cows and the calves and so on. So they left about 30 or 40 behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were put into those buses. And we of course knew that our next stop will be Dachau, Buchenwald, or Mauthausen. It was very desperate. Very, very desperate.

speaker: interviewer
text: I told my friend, Rudi Katz, who was much older, who was a judge in Breslau, Germany, and I said to him, well, did that sound pretty good? He says, well, when we are in the concentration camp, they will sound differently. And then he said to me, Eric, let me give you one advice. He was in Dachau before.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, give me one advice. When you are in concentration camp, never be the first in front. Never be the last. Don't wear glasses. Don't talk loud. Play dead, because blend like an object into the background. Nobody should hear or see you. Well, we didn't come to Dachau. We came to the diamond factory of Asscher. There is a big diamond industry in Holland, and one of the kings of the diamond industry was someone-- someone by the name of Asscher. We came to his factory, and they opened the doors of the buses-- oh-- and out we went. And then we were accepted by many Dutch families. They said, well, I take that girl. I take that boy. It was pre-arranged because the Jewish council in Amsterdam was aware of that. And so 350 or whatever of the boys and girls from that camp were taken in by Dutch families in Amsterdam. And so was I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that Abraham Asscher who you were talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he-- he was the head of the Jewish Council?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He and Professor Cohen were the leaders of the Jewish Council, what you would call here the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jewish Council, the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Federation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Federation Council, we call it here. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you worked in the diamond factory? You said--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's only where the buses were unloaded. I was-- I came to a family by the name of Kaufmann, on Raphaelstraat 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and what did you do every day while you  were living with the Kaufmann family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. However, it was not important because on June 11, 1941, which is about two months later, something else happened. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I left my house, Raphaelstraat 20, right around 3:00 in the afternoon, and I was visiting [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a family by the name of Lehman. There were three boys. The two sons-- Lehman, they were in [? Viering ?] and they went to their parents. The two boys, their parents were living in Amsterdam, so they were not taken in by another family. They were the two Lehman boys and one other guy from [? Viering ?] by the name of Ansel Freidlander. And I went visiting with them. And after 20 minutes, the doorbell rang, and a maid comes in with two Gestapo people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your name? Gert Lehman. Over there. What's your name? Kurt Lehman. Looking at the list, over there. What's your name? Ansel Friedlander. Over there. What your name? Eric [? Felgenheimer. ?] Not on this list. They had me on the list at Raphaelstraat where I lived, and I left. Not on the list.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, here I belong. They go out on street. Name of the street is [INAUDIBLE]. And I see another young boy from [? Viering ?] by the name of Kurt Wurzweiler with his head down, walking on street between two policemen-- Dutch policeman. And I saw another one and another one. I don't know what to do. I go to my cousin, Julius [INAUDIBLE], Beethovenstraat 106. Now, it's around 5:00, 6:00. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I come to my cousin and only his wife was there. And she looks at me and she says, Eric, you're so smart. You always come shortly before dinner.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, that's right. So I was sitting there and talking. In the meantime, her husband came home, and her son. Name of the son was Heinz. And they all said to me, Eric, you are so-- so quiet. You usually talk so. What's wrong? There's something wrong. I told them what happened. Oh, that's terrible. Thank God nothing happened to our son and to you. Minute later, a neighbor came in-- young woman-- and said, they're going into houses and take out Jewish boys. Get rid of Heinz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my cousin picked up the telephone, called a non-Jewish friend, and said, can I send over my son? They're going into houses, taking out the Jewish boys. By the way, the same thing happened in the central Amsterdam in February of '41, the big situation where they also took about 400. Anyway, my cousin called a friend, talked about Heinz. Nobody talked about me. I felt very, very lonely. So Heinz and I, we went down and into-- there was an elevator. We walked down the stairs, was second or third floor. And I said to Heinz, Heinz, lets divide the risk. One goes left. One goes right. Which way do you go? He said, well, my bicycle is on the left, so I'll go to the left. He did. 30 to 40 yards later, I heard the sque-- squeaking of some brakes. I turned around. Two men jumped out of a car and grabbed Heinz, took him in the car. And I was there. Again, they didn't take me. So I went-- in the meantime the-- those details, are they important? Yes, tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kurtznelson, the Director of the camp, was-- he was a British citizen. He was a citizen of Israel, which was at that time Palestine. So he had a British passport. He was taking an internship and we had another guy, Professor Abel Hertzberg. He became the-- the head of the Werkdorp Nieuwesluis, which did not exist anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't know where to go, so I went to him. And he made a phone call to a family by the name of Birnbaum. He was Jewish, and she was not. And he said, can I send some over there to sleep? And they said yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came there. And I stayed there are a few days. And the daughter of the Birnbaums-- her name was Ilse-- was very friendly with the girl by the name of Laura Pintus. That's the one I fell in love with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Laura.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the one I got engaged to. That-- now it really gets-- very bad things are happening. And I moved in with them. In the ensuing months-- now we are middle of June. Between middle of June and middle of September, every day, two or three or four or one or five announcement of death of those boys came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was 800 boys, 400 arrested in February in central [INAUDIBLE], 400 approximately arrested in Amsterdam [? Sud. ?] They were killed in Mauthausen. And do you know how they got killed? How did they get killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Would you like to know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Are you sure you want to know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: How do you call that, where you take-- I don't know the English word-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where you take rock out of the mountain? Stones.

speaker: interviewer
text: A quarry. A quarry.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the word. There was next to the camp was a big quarry, and the boys had to walk down these stairs very heavily loaded with rocks. And they were beaten with whips while they were walking down the quarry. And most of them-- three, four, or five at the time-- they were holding hands and were jumping down to their death. Committed suicide. And the camp guards, they called them [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] parachutists.

speaker: interviewer
text: Parastists?

speaker: interviewee
text: Parachute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, parachutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they overwhelmed them, so they committed suicide by doing that. And I didn't get there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So they all get killed. And in the meantime, everything in Amsterdam became impossible. And eventually, we went underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you go underground with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the meantime, I stayed with the Pintus family. By the way, the brother of Laura was also arrested-- my-- my fiancee, her brother, Heinz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he taken to Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And they took me in as a son. And, well, things became very bad. They were continuously arrests and murders and then beatings. And-- and, I mean, it's all well-known. And in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in July '42 exactly, the deportations from Jews from Holland into Auschwitz and Sobibor started. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, I have to tell you something. I wished I wouldn't, but I will. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My-- the Pintus family, she had a very good friend, lady friend in England, and their daughter, Ilse, lived in Holland. And she was about the same age like Laura. Now, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I took a job as a typist at the Jewish Council in Amsterdam. And therefore, I was pretty much safe for the time being of being arrested because I was a typist for the Jewish Council. Anyway, one evening Ilse, the daughter of Mrs. Pintus's lady friend in London came. And she says, well, I got here my-- my whatchamacallit-- my invitation to be tonight, at 8:00, at Grand Central Station to go into train to there and there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I said to Mrs. Pintus, let's talk for a moment. I say, we have to do something. We cannot let her go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We have to find a hiding place for her. And she says, no, we cannot for certain reasons. She said, I'm not going to say why. She said she's too dumb and she brings us all in danger. So Ilse left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hour and a half later, she comes back. She had the wrong date. She was supposed to go tomorrow.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, I talked with Mrs. Pintus to save her. No. I had no license to do it. And so finally, I convinced [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the parents of Laura that we have to go underground. And we should be-- the young people should go somewhere and the-- the old people should go somewhere. And we needed false papers. So I called the Secretary of Mr. Pintus in his office and I said to her, your husband is a big man in the underground. Get me some false papers. And we got that false ID, which I've shown you a moment ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Peter Fensler. And Laura and I went to Voorburg, which is a suburb of The Hague. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were living there in the apartment of a young girl from Suriname-- that's West Indies-- who was a telephone operator. And it was terrible. Because we couldn't talk in daytime. We couldn't go to the toilet. We couldn't flush the water because the neighbors would hear there's somebody in the apartment, and there was nobody there. So in the evening it was a little bit different. And we got there in November-- in late July, early August. And Laura and I, we had to separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did-- I mean, how did you know to go where you went? Who told you to go there? You went to this apartment in-- in the Dutch West Indies, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: But who told you to go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the secretary. The office secretary of-- not she, her  husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took care of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the-- the old Pintuses-- I mean, the parents-- they went to [INAUDIBLE], and the [INAUDIBLE] on 47 in Amsterdam. And that was her neighbors. And they went there. They had 8,000-- my-- the-- my so-called father-in-law had 8,000 guilders. He gave me four, and he kept four. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Laura and I got into big fights. And then, in November, I couldn't take it anymore and I left. And Laura went to her parents' and I went somewhere else. Oy. [LAUGHS] Should I go on? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- the fights what I had with Laura all about, they're not important. I'm not going into that. That's not important. Anyway, here I'm in Amsterdam. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I have no money. I gave the $4,000 back to Mr. Pintus. I had no place to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop and just change the tape.
